data.title: Altria: Potential Beyond Traditional Tobacco Products Makes It A Must-Own For Dividend Investors
data.description:
Introduction

Most of us are well aware of the risks from the consistent and steady decline in smokeable products. And while this will continue to pose a threat to Altria's (NYSE:MO) dividend safety and peers alike, their robust growth in their smokeless products makes them a compelling dividend stock passive income investors should look to own.

Like every investment, owning a company at the right price is essential, and Altria, despite being up double-digits over the past year, could be poised to see further upside over the next 12–24 months due to additional growth drivers discussed later in the article.

During their latest earnings, Altria delivered what, I thought, was a solid earnings report to close out the year. And in this article, I discuss their latest report, financials, and why potential drivers of growth make them a must own stock for passive income investors.

Previous Thesis

I last covered Altria back in a November article titled: Altria: Sit Back And Continue To NJOY The Dividends. Since then, the stock has remained relatively flat, down slightly less than 1% in comparison to the S&P who has also seen a somewhat flat performance. Up less than 1%, the overall market seems to be dealing with uncertainty surrounding inflation.

As you can see in the chart below, MO continued their solid performance, trading near $58 a share before retracting back at the end of November and trading rangebound since.

Although smokeable products continued their steady decline during the third quarter, Altria showed strong price appreciation due to their strong growth of NJOY & on!

This allowed management to beat on both their top and bottom lines. EPS amounted to $1.38, beating estimates by $0.03 while revenue of $5.34 billion beat by a solid $10 million.

The cigarette giant's balance sheet was also strong with leverage of 2.1x, offsetting smokeable challenges and enhancing their dividend safety. Moreover, at a then forward P/E of 10.54x, Altria offered significant upside with a price target of roughly $74 if they returned to their normal P/E according to Fast Graphs.

Robust Smokeless Category Growth

During their most recent quarter, announced at the end of January, Altria again managed to beat on their top and bottom lines.

EPS of $1.29 declined from $1.38 in the previous quarter but beat analysts' estimates by a penny. Revenue of $5.1 billion also declined from $5.4 billion the previous quarter but beat estimates by a solid $50 million.

Peer Philip Morris (PM) saw a significantly higher beat by $270 million, driven by strong growth in their oral tobacco category.

As expected, smokeable products continued to trend downward, with revenues declining 0.2% and domestic volumes declining 10.2% for the full year. This was driven by expected lower shipment volumes, partially offset by higher pricing as seen in the chart below.

And while this will likely continue to pose risks for Altria for the foreseeable future, the company's robust growth in their smokeless products like NJOY & on! continues to show promise.

Other good news is that according to Altria's management, the number of adult consumers in the e-vapor and oral categories grew to roughly 28 million, on par with adult traditional smokers.

In the chart below, you can see the growth in revenue per capita expected over the next 5 years for e-cigarettes. This is expected to grow near 22% from 2025 to 2029.

And using Altria's distribution network, I expect both NJOY and on! to continue to see market share growth for the foreseeable future. Last year's fourth quarter saw Altria manage to distribute NJOY to over 75,000 stores, greatly enhancing their visibility and engagement with consumers.

Today, NJOY is in more than 100,000 stores. And volumes grew 15.3% year-over-year as a result to 12.8 million units and 46.6 million units respectively for the full-year. Devices saw even higher growth of more than 22% while consumables retail share increased 2.8 points from the prior year.

Oral nicotine pouches grew 9.6 points as a result of shipment volumes, which were up an impressive 44% with cans increasing from 30.4 million to 43.9 million.

For comparison purposes, Philip Morris saw shipment volumes grow from roughly 220 million to 275 million cans over the same period. Moreover, on! significantly trailed ZYN in terms of market share at 8.9% in comparison to 65.9%.

Potential Growth Drivers

And continuing their Moving Beyond Smoking campaign, management is still working towards their PTMA submission for Ploom through their joint venture with Japan Tobacco Inc. (OTCPK:JAPAY).

This is expected to be submitted by mid-2025. Helix also saw their first profitable quarter in Q4, and management expects this to continue going forward. And both could become significant growth drivers, resulting in strong price appreciation in the future.

So, although declines in smokeable products continue to weigh negatively on the company's share price, so does the large disparity in market share in comparison to Philip Morris' ZYN. But Altria seems to be on the right track to offset the continued decline in traditional smoking with volume and market share growth.

Earnings Growth & Buybacks

Altria's relentless share repurchases are another reason why they remain a favorite amongst dividend investors. And while this also helps grow their bottom line and somewhat offset the secular decline in traditional smoking, it also greatly enhances their dividend safety by saving millions annually by retiring shares.

Earnings were able to see solid growth of 9.3% from $1.18 to $1.29 year-over-year. This was in comparison to Philip Morris, who's earnings grew double-digits from $1.36 to $1.55 a share.

And while PM continues to outperform Altria in terms of price appreciation and smokeless tobacco product growth, Altria's frequent buybacks enables them to successfully navigate smoking challenges while returning ample cash to shareholders.

The company recently announced an additional $1 billion repurchase program that they intend to use by the end of the year. And EPS growth for 2025 is expected to be 3.4% from this year's $5.12 to their midpoint of $5.295.

And while this is low in comparison to PM's expectation of high single-digit growth over the next two years, Altria's yield and commitment to return cash to shareholders make them worth owning.

For 2024, the cigarette giant returned a total of $10.2 billion to shareholders, $6.8 billion of this in dividends. This was a much higher increase from $7.8 billion returned in 2023.

And still, free cash flow was sufficient enough to cover the $6.8 billion in dividends paid. With management repurchasing another $1 billion worth of shares this year, Altria's dividend remains safe for the near to medium term.

Debt

Altria's debt has also remained somewhat flat over the past few years. In 2022, long-term debt stood at $25.1 billion and roughly the same in 2023. Currently, this stands at $24.93 billion, and they had the lowest leverage ratio of their peer group at 2.1x. Philip Morris' net debt to EBITDA ratio was 2.66x, while British American Tobacco's (BTI) leverage was 2.6x.

Strong Total Returns/Risks

At a forward P/E of just 10.1x, Altria's dividend yield of nearly 8% and upside potential makes them a worthy investment for those in search of passive income. The stock has also significantly outperformed the S&P in total returns over the past year, delivering a solid 44% in comparison to 21% for the index.

Moreover, if interest rates decline this year, Altria should benefit, potentially seeing some upside by the end of the year. However, current risks from inflation as well as their lawsuit with JUUL will likely limit upside for the near term.

An order to stop the sale of NJOY ACE products was issued, ruling in JUUL's favor. MO's management appealed and is awaiting a decision that is expected to come in April of this year. If the decision is upheld, this could also result in downside for Altria.

Upside Potential

As stated previously, Altria has significant upside potential, especially if interest rates are lowered and the company is able to continue to see solid growth in NJOY & on! as well as additional growth drivers with Helix and Ploom.

Moreover, their dividend yield of nearly 8% is attractive and investors looking to get back into the market due to less appealing, fixed-rate investments will likely look to buy into the stock.

Using an expected 3% earnings growth rate, in-line with the past few years and 2.5% thereafter to account for any slowdowns in the economy or Altria's business, the cigarette giant offers solid upside of 16.7% at the time of writing.

Since Altria is a mature business and a higher yielding investment, I used an 11% expected return, slightly higher than the S&P's 7% - 10% historical average. However, as stated previously, upside will rely heavily on the direction of interest rates as well as their pending lawsuit.

Bottom Line

Altria, despite a strong performance, up over 32% in the past year, offers dividend investors great value with a forward multiple of less than 11x earnings.

And although current risks will likely weigh on the company's share price in the near term, on! and NJOY share growth continue to show Altria is positioning themselves for the consumer shift to smokeless tobacco products.

Moreover, potential growth drivers like Ploom and Helix could also be catalysts for the stock to see solid price appreciation over the long term along with the continued decline in interest rates. Altria's commitment to shareholder returns, attractive valuation, and potential growth drivers makes them a long-term buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 3 Of My Best Dividend Picks For A Huge Economic Shift
data.description:
Introduction

A 91% price surge since 2019. That sounds like horrible inflation, doesn't it?

Luckily, I'm talking about the Audemars Piguet price index, which tracks the weighted average price of this super-expensive Swiss watch brand. If you want a decent entry model and avoid the retail waitlist, you'll probably have to pay close to $50,000 (without any limits to the upside).

The good news is that this is the kind of inflation nobody really cares about. While it may be annoying for some that their favorite watch is now way more expensive, this isn't the kind of inflation that hurts communities.

If anything, it's a great indicator of market liquidity, as prices went through the roof when central bank liquidity propelled global asset prices during the pandemic. Since the Fed started hiking rates, that party has ended.

I'm bringing all of this up because not all inflation is equal.

While higher watch prices may be annoying, inflation in areas like food, housing, and energy, can be critical for many. The other day, I talked to someone who has been trying to find a new suitable house for him and his girlfriend for more than a year, as almost everything that isn't out of their financial reach comes with what seems to be unlimited buyer competition.

For example, all-item inflation seems to be bottoming at 3%. Rent inflation is still at 4%, a number even above the already elevated pre-pandemic levels.

Unfortunately, new data suggests that this could get worse, as my "higher for longer" thesis seems to get support from unfavorable housing developments - at least for renters and everyone looking to get into the market.

I believe the headline below speaks for itself:

After two years of falling inflation rates (not falling prices), the housing market is expected to see a toxic mix of low homeownership affordability, a rapidly depleting new construction pipeline, and elevated interest rates.

This will be a tough pill to swallow for consumers and another burden on the Fed, which is already in a tough spot due to rebounding inflation rates.

Rising rents would complicate the inflation picture and likely give the Federal Reserve another reason to pause on future rate cuts. Shelter costs account for roughly a third of the consumer-price index, which means that a significant portion of the overall inflation measure is attributed to housing costs.

“It will be very hard for the headline inflation number to reach the Fed’s 2% goal without a slowdown in housing costs,” said Lisa Sturtevant, chief economist at Bright MLS. - WSJ (emphasis added)

According to the Journal (which cites CoStar data), every single metropolitan market is expected to see positive rent growth by the end of this year.

As you can imagine, this has a number of implications:

  • Rent inflation will likely accelerate.
  • Much more new housing supply is needed to alleviate these pressures.
  • Politicians may push for looser regulations.
  • Labor demand will rise.

With all of this in mind, in light of these key developments, I present three dividend stocks that, I believe, will benefit tremendously from this.

I truly believe that they are among the best companies to invest in moving forward, as each brings something unique to the table, including innovative business models that benefit from more than just favorable macroeconomic trends.

While I do not own any of them, I am currently buying one for family accounts and likely for my own account after dealing with taxes and other matters in the next 4–6 weeks. The other two are on my watchlist.

So, let's dive into the picks!

Equity LifeStyle Properties (ELS) - I Believe It's Perfect

I have increasingly covered ELS this year, as I am starting to become a bit obsessed with the stock - in a good way.

We are currently buying ELS for family accounts. I will likely buy the stock after my trip to the U.S. when I have more clarity regarding taxes and issues I may have to deal with. Needless to say, I'll keep readers up to date.

For now, I want to do what I did in my February 9 article about suitable retirement stocks, which is to explain why Equity LifeStyle Properties is such a wickedly cool company.

Equity LifeStyle Properties is one of the best-performing REITs on the market, as it returned roughly 15% per year since going public, putting it in the best 10% of all public REITs over the past ten years.

The company, which has gone sideways since the pandemic, is the second-largest owner of manufactured housing ("MH") communities in the United States. It also owns RV communities and marinas. Put together, it generates 90% of its revenues from annual sources.

Even better, a big part of its 452 properties cater to the elderly. This is a huge benefit, as that group looks for affordable living arrangements with supportive amenities in places like California, Arizona, and Florida. It is also a fast-growing customer group due to the "Silver Tsunami." Between 2024 and 2039, the number of 55-year-olds in the U.S. is expected to rise by 15%.

Moreover, one major benefit of ELS is the increasing attractiveness of MH communities. Because of the increasing quality of manufactured houses and the company's amenities, MH communities are a real alternative for many people.

While upfront costs are usually higher, we see that total monthly costs are up to 70% lower for the typical MH compared to a single-family home.

But wait, it gets better.

On top of demand tailwinds, MH operators have supply tailwinds, which is quite unique in the real estate sector. As we can see below, supply has been constrained since 2008. Strict zoning and regulation have kept a lid on new developments, which is great for pricing power.

Additionally, 77% of ELS residents have a FICO score of more than 680. It has an occupancy rate of 95% with a homeownership ratio of almost 98%. This means that most people own their MH assets outright. They do not pay rent but monthly lot fees for the use of amenities.

Because of these qualities, the company has tremendous inflation protection, as most of its tenants have inflation-protected income and MH communities become increasingly more attractive when single-family affordability falls.

Since 2001, its net operating income growth has beaten inflation by almost 2.0% per year, which is a big deal.

The same goes for its dividend. Currently, ELS yields 3.2%. This yield comes with a five-year CAGR of 12.6% and an 80% payout ratio. Its most recent dividend hike was 7.9% on February 6.

Moreover, it has a healthy balance sheet with a net leverage ratio of just 4.6x EBITDA, an average of nine years to maturity, and a weighted average interest rate of 3.8%.

Looking ahead, analysts expect 4-6% average annual per-share adjusted funds from operations ("AFFO") growth. In other words, even without valuation tailwinds, the company could return close to 10%.

However, as ELS trades roughly four points below its 10-year average, it could return substantially more. Needless to say, I expect we need lower interest rates for that to happen.

Nonetheless, I believe the long-term risk/reward is fantastic, especially in light of more housing inflation.

While ELS does not yield 6% like some of the REITs you may have in your portfolio or on your radar, I believe it's one of the best dividend stocks money can buy - across all sectors.

D.R. Horton (DHI) - A Special Builder

There are two homebuilders I really like.

  • NVR (NVR).
  • D.R. Horton.

In this article, I'm covering D.R. Horton, as NVR does not pay a dividend.

NVR also does not have that one big benefit that D.R. Horton has, which is a big focus on affordable homes. It has also been the biggest homebuilder in America for 23 consecutive years, with a major footprint in 36 states.

Because demand for affordable homes has grown at a fast pace, the company's market share has risen from less than 3% in the early 1990s to almost 15% in 2024. This includes being the biggest builder in 61 markets, including many in its home state, Texas.

What's fascinating is that nearly 70% of its homes that were sold over the past four quarters had a price tag of less than $400,000. A quarter of its homes were delivered for less than $300,000. The median home price in the United States is north of $410,000.

On top of that, the company has mastered the art of building homes efficiently. After all, selling homes at a below-average price isn't rocket science. Doing it without sacrificing margins is where it becomes complicated.

However, the company beats its peers on three key metrics:

  • Return on equity.
  • Return on assets.
  • The 10-year total stock return.

During its 1Q25 (fiscal year) earnings call, the company noted that despite economic challenges, housing market conditions remain favorable for homebuilders, driven by strong demographic trends and the limited supply of affordable homes.

In order to deal with affordability concerns and to boost demand, D.R. Horton implemented targeted incentives, which included mortgage rate buy-downs and a strategic shift toward smaller floor plans.

With regard to its dividend, the company yields 1.2%. This dividend has a five-year CAGR of 17% and a payout ratio of a mere 9%. The reason behind that is the company's focus on buybacks, which have exceeded dividend payments by a wide margin since at least 2020.

While this dividend may be a bit disappointing, the total return has been fantastic over the past decade, as the company has consistently beaten the S&P 500 with total returns in the highest quartiles.

Valuation-wise, D.R. Horton trades at 9.5x earnings, a few points below its long-term average of 11.1x. While this fiscal year is expected to see an 11% EPS contraction, 2026 and 2027 are expected to see 12% and 13% EPS growth, respectively.

Personally, I believe these numbers will be higher if President Trump can avoid a recession and even lower rates a bit from current levels. In general, if the urge to build more homes grows, D.R. Horton is a very likely winner.

I believe this could unlock a fair stock price target of at least $180, roughly 37-40% above the current price and based on 2027 EPS expectations and its 11.1x long-term average P/E ratio.

The biggest risks are rising rates and Trump's mass deportations. Regardless of one's views on illegal immigration (I'm not getting political), deporting illegal labor could hurt the construction industry, as it's a source of cheap labor.

With that said, the next pick is similar, yet different due to a different place in the supply chain.

Boise Cascade Company (BCC) - Buying High-Quality Distribution

Boise Cascade is a company I would have bought if I had not already had a large position in its peer, Carlisle Companies (CSL), which I bought after the pandemic.

Boise Cascade is a fascinating company that benefits tremendously from inflation and housing shortages. Over the past five years, it has returned 332%, almost three times the return of the homebuilding ETF (ITB).

This company does not build homes. It's a supplier with a strong internal supply chain that includes two sawmills, 11 plywood/veneer plants, and 38 distribution facilities - among other key assets.

It uses its distribution facilities to sell its products to builders, remodelers, do-it-yourself customers, and industrial players. However, it is more than just a company that turns trees into building products, as it is increasingly expanding its distribution network to sell everything needed to build homes and other construction projects. This includes a wide range of brands that are all part of the wider distribution channel, as we can see below.

In 2023, the company generated $1.93 billion in revenues from its wood products division, with an EBITDA margin of 22.6%. That's up from 8.8% in 2019 and a confirmation of years of investments in higher efficiencies.

In its building materials segment, it generated close to $7.0 billion in sales, supported by a 9.9% average annual revenue growth since 2013. On top of secular growth tailwinds, it also benefits from size, as the company supplied 5% of all multi-family wood floors in 2023 and 13% of all multi-family I-joists, to give you two numbers.

Generally speaking, the company's focus on higher quality products is paying off, as the building industry is increasingly focused on products that reduce labor efforts (think of current deportations), improve cycle times, increase strength, and improve sustainability.

As much as I love builders, I love the distribution business, as it covers an entirely different area of the supply chain. I also expect AI and similar technologies to provide massive opportunities for efficiency improvements in this industry.

With regard to its dividend, BCC pays $0.21 per share per quarter. This translates to a yield of 0.7%. This dividend has an 8% payout ratio and a five-year CAGR of 17.3%.

However, it gets better, as the company pays special dividends.

Last year, it announced a $5.00 special dividend, which brings the total annualized yield to 4.8%. Although this special dividend is volatile, it has consistently risen over time, as we can see below.

Valuation-wise, we're dealing with a company trading at 12.6x earnings, below its ten-year average of 14.5x. Going forward, analysts expect 8%, 7%, and 17% EPS growth in 2025, 2026, and 2027, respectively.

This implies a fair stock price of $186, roughly 50% above the current price.

While all of these numbers are subject to change, I believe tailwinds are strong and provide a favorable risk/reward - even if interest rates remain elevated.

As I already said, the only reason I do not own BCC is because I own CSL. Because I will have to consolidate some positions before adding new investments, I decided to avoid buying more stocks in the same industry.

Takeaway

Not all inflation is created equal.

While luxury items like (Audemars Piguet) watches have seen staggering price surges, it's the inflation in essentials like housing, food, and energy that truly impacts lives.

The housing market in particular is facing a toxic mix of low affordability, falling supply, and high rates, which could keep rent inflation elevated.

Against this backdrop, I've identified three dividend stocks: Equity LifeStyle Properties, D.R. Horton, and Boise Cascade.

Each offers unique strengths, from affordable housing solutions to efficient supply chain distribution.

While I don't own all of them yet, I'm actively adding ELS to family accounts and keeping a close eye on the others.

Risks To My Thesis

Rising interest rates could further pressure housing affordability and slow demand, while economic downturns or policy changes (like mass deportations impacting construction labor) could disrupt supply chains and growth.

Additionally, if inflation in housing costs cools faster than expected or regulatory hurdles ease, the tailwinds driving these stocks may weaken.

Lastly, valuation multiples could remain depressed if market sentiment sours, meaning general market weakness could hurt these stocks.

data.title: Why Pfizer's Stock Price Isn't Reflecting Its True Value
data.description:
On February 4, Pfizer (NYSE:PFE) (NEOE:PFE:CA) released financial results for the fourth quarter of 2024, which again beat my expectations and those of Wall Street analysts.

Investment thesis

Pfizer, under the leadership of Albert Bourla, remains under pressure from critics, pessimists, and others, which is not surprising since its stock price is near multi-year lows, and Oxbryta, which was gaining popularity for the treatment of sickle cell anemia, was withdrawn from the market.

But is it really that bad?

Of course not.

In this article, you will not only read about the performance of its oncology, migraine, and cardiovascular franchises and its latest successes in developing a portfolio of experimental drugs, but also learn why I continue to cover Pfizer with a 'Strong Buy' rating.

Pfizer continues to beat Wall Street expectations, but its share price remains flat despite progress in R&D

Pfizer revenue was about $17.8 billion in the fourth quarter of 2024, up 24.9% year over year and 0.6% quarter over quarter.

In addition, looking at historical data, it has beaten consensus estimates by relatively large margins in each of the last four quarters, even as sales of Xeljanz [Janus kinase inhibitor] continue to decline due to the loss of its exclusivity in Canada and increased competition from AbbVie's Rinvoq (ABBV), Novartis' Cosentyx (NVS), and Johnson & Johnson's Stelara (JNJ).

Then, dear Seeking Alpha readers, you may have the first question, namely, what factors contributed to the beat of Wall Street analysts and my expectations?

So, Pfizer's revenue growth was driven by a sharp increase in sales of COVID-19 products [Comirnaty and Paxlovid], as well as increased demand for its oncology and tafamidis franchises.

Since Pfizer is most interested in oncology, it is logical that I will continue the discussion with an analysis of this franchise.

Sales of its oncology franchise reached about $4.06 billion in the fourth quarter of 2024, up 27.4% year-on-year and 0.5% quarter-on-quarter, mainly due to increased demand for its antibody-drug-conjugates, Xtandi, Elrexfio, Braftovi/Mektovi, and Elrexfio, which is a BCMA/CD3 bispecific antibody approved by the FDA in mid-August 2023 for the treatment of multiple myeloma.

The greatest weight in terms of sales relative to the revenue of this portfolio belongs to the medications that were acquired as part of the deal with Seagen.

So, the total sales of Tukysa, Tivdak, Adcetris, and Padcev amounted to $894 million, which is 7.1% more than in Q3 2024 and 92.7% more than in Q4 2022. Also, I would like to note that it was incorrect to compare the dynamics relative to the fourth quarter of 2023 because Pfizer, in its report, indicated only the sales of these 4 drugs for the period from the closing of the deal until the end of the year.

In my assessment, Xtandi (enzalutamide), which is a second-generation androgen receptor inhibitor [ARI], has also become a significant contributor to strengthening the balance sheet of the New York-based company.

Sales of the medication, developed jointly with Astellas (OTCPK:ALPMF), totaled $565 million in the fourth quarter, up 79.9% year-on-year, primarily due to its NMPA approval in early July 2024 for the treatment of patients with mHSPC [metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer].

The second factor contributing to the growth in demand for it was the approval by the EMA on January 8, 2024, in combination with Talzenna [PARP inhibitor]. The positive decision of the regulatory authority was based on the Phase 3 TALAPRO-2 study, in which the group of patients taking this combination had a 37% lower risk of death/disease progression compared to the placebo+Xtandi.

Moreover, on February 13, 2025, Pfizer reported additional clinical data that also pleased me because, in two cohorts of this pivotal trial, which has been going on for more than five years, Xtandi plus Talzenna led to a statistically significant increase in median overall survival relative to the combination of Xtandi with placebo.

Anticipating your question, dear Seeking Alpha readers. Who is the flagship in Pfizer's Specialty Care segment?

Of course, it is the tafamidis franchise. As I mentioned in the article "Pfizer: Tracking Well Above The Industry," it consists of three drugs, namely Vyndaqel [global], Vyndamax [U.S.], and Vynmac [Japan], which are approved for the treatment of cardiomyopathy. In simple terms, cardiomyopathy is a progressive disease that makes it harder for the heart to pump blood.

So, sales of the Vyndaqel family reached about $1.55 billion in Q4 2024, which is 60.8% more than a year earlier and 127.2% more than in the same period of 2022. This result beat my expectations by $120 million, since I believed that tougher competition from Alnylam Pharmaceuticals' Onpattro (ALNY) and BridgeBio Pharma's Attruby (BBIO) would somewhat slow demand for Pfizer's blockbuster.

From its Primary Care segment, I highlight Nurtec ODT/Vydura (rimegepant), a medication for the treatment of various types of migraines, which partially minimized the decline in sales of the RSV vaccine called Abrysvo.

I will indicate the reasons for the decline in demand for Abrysvo a little later, namely in the "risks" section.

Nurtec ODT/Vydura sales amounted to $392 million for the 3 months ended December 31, 2024, increasing by 39% year-on-year and by 16.3% quarter-on-quarter due to strong demand for it in the United States, including due to its competitive advantages relative to Amgen's Aimovig (AMGN), as well as its launch in international markets.

Let's continue with a discussion of the financial metric that I prefer more, namely earnings per share [EPS].

It also did not disappoint me, even though Pfizer continues to increase investments in the development of its FDA-approved and experimental medications, especially for those indications where there remain high unmet needs, including metastatic breast cancer, advanced solid tumors, metastatic non-small cell lung cancer, and lupus.

Which experimental drugs and vaccines do I single out as those that could restore Wall Street's faith in Pfizer's bright future as early as 2025?

From the list of its product candidates, which you can also see below, I highlight sasanlimab [bladder cancer], atirmociclib [breast cancer], vepdegestrant [breast cancer], and danuglipron [chronic weight management and type 2 diabetes], which, in my estimation, have significant commercial potential and could once again make Wall Street think about Pfizer in a positive way.

In addition, I want to highlight two points that pleased me as a Pfizer investor.

First, it terminated its partnership with Sangamo Therapeutics (SGMO) to develop giroctocogene fitelparvovec. I believe this decision was correct since this gene therapy for hemophilia A has demonstrated similar efficacy as BioMarin's Roctavian (BMRN), but its sales were poor, amounting to only $7 million in the third quarter of 2024.

Also, I believe that due to the long-acting medications, including Sanofi's Altuviiio (SNY) and Roche Holding's Hemlibra (OTCQX:RHHBY), it is unlikely that the medical community will choose to use expensive gene therapies.

The second point that strengthened my confidence in Pfizer's bright future is the words of Dave Denton, its CFO, on the Q4 earnings call. So, he said that the company has at least $10 billion that it could use for mergers and acquisitions.

However, dear Seeking Alpha readers, let's get back to discussing the dynamics of changes in Pfizer's EPS.

Its non-GAAP earnings per share [non-GAAP EPS] was 63 cents, up more than five times year-over-year and beating the analysts' consensus estimates by 17 cents. One factor that contributed to this was the more than successful implementation of a cost realignment program that began in 2023.

So, on the Q4 earnings call, Dave Denton, stated the following.

Importantly, we believe the steps taken to right size our cost base will put us on a strong footing towards increased operational efficiency and support our goal to return to pre-pandemic operating margins. In further support of this initiative, we now expect to deliver overall net savings of $4.5 billion from our ongoing cost realignment programs by the end of 2025, while continuing to advance programs that will improve cost of goods sold in the years to come.

Thanks to the wide range of data provided through Seeking Alpha, each of its readers, including me, can look a little into the future and assess how specific financial metrics of Pfizer will change.

Wall Street analysts forecast its non-GAAP EPS to be between 54 and 81 cents for the first three months of 2025, implying 12.7% quarterly growth. For comparison, I expect it to be 77 cents, which is above the midpoint of the above range due to stronger sales of antibody-drug conjugates used to treat various types of cancer, Nurtec ODT/Vydura, Zavicefta, Sulperazon and Cibinqo.

Risks

In the table below, I have highlighted the key risks related to Pfizer that, in my opinion, each of you, namely Seeking Alpha readers, should consider.

Takeaway

Dear Seeking Alpha readers, in this article, I presented an analysis of Pfizer's results for Q4 2024, my thoughts on its R&D policy, and successes and failures in developing an oncology franchise.

However, that's not all.

In addition to its high dividend yield of 6.74%, another factor that makes me a Pfizer investor and also why it is an attractive asset for long-term investors is that it trades at multiples that are significantly lower than those of its key competitors, including AstraZeneca (AZN) and Eli Lilly (LLY).

So, it trades at a P/E ratio [TTM] of 8.21x, which is 54.5% lower than the sector median and 23.4% lower than the average over the past 5 years. Besides, I estimate Pfizer's non-GAAP EPS to grow to $3.26, which corresponds to a P/E ratio of 7.33x in 2029.

data.title: Winter 2025 Snapshot Of Expected Future S&P 500 Earnings
data.description:
Every three months, we take a snapshot of the expectations for future earnings in the S&P 500 (SPX) at approximately the midpoint of the current quarter, shortly after most U.S. firms have announced their previous quarter's earnings.

The latest snapshot is a little early, but still 90 days since the Fall 2024 snapshot. During this time, remarkably little changed in the outlook for the collective earnings of the companies that make up the S&P 500 index. That relative lack of change is a new development that's taken place over the past six months.

That's remarkable because it runs counter to the pattern we typically see in how the outlook for earnings changes with time. That pattern is one in which the expectations for future earnings tend to erode with each later snapshot.

To be sure, that pattern holds in the Winter 2025 snapshot, but the amount of erosion is tiny. Looking at the S&P 500's anticipated earnings per share for the end of the fourth quarter of 2025, we find those expectations dipped by $1.49 per share. That's a decline of just 0.6%.

The following chart, covering how earnings expectations have changed from the end of 2021 through 11 February 2025 illustrates both the typical pattern and the relative lack of change in those expectations since 13 August 2024:

The current projection for the S&P 500's earnings per share through the end of 2025 is $249.13, which would represent 18.2% year-over-year earnings growth over December 2024's level of $210.81. Given the typical pattern for how earnings projections develop over time, that figure represents the likely ceiling for potential earnings growth during 2025.

The Winter 2025 snapshot also includes the first projection of the index's expected earnings per share through the end of 2026. The first estimate of what those earnings will be is $289.64 per share.

Reference

Silverblatt, Howard. Standard & Poor's. S&P 500 Earnings and Estimates. 13 November 2024. Accessed 17 February 2025.

Original Post

data.title: Euro's Pullback Extends, While The Kiwi Shrugs Off Third Half-Point Cut By The RBNZ
data.description:
Overview

The US warned that the next phase of the tariff war could be announced in early April and three industries, auto, pharma, and chips. President Trump's comments suggested sometime between the announcement and the implementation. He did not specify if USMCA trade would be exempt from the auto tariffs. Some auto parts reportedly cross the border four-five times. The dollar is mixed against the G10 currencies. The Reserve Bank of New Zealand delivered its third consecutive 50 bps rate cut and the Kiwi's 0.25% gain puts it at the top of the G10 today. Among emerging market currencies, all but a couple Asian currencies are lower, with central European currencies, the softest.

Equities are heavier. Except for China and South Korea, the large bourses in Asia Pacific were fell today. Europe's Stoxx 600 (STOXX) has stalled after rising for six of the past seven sessions. US index futures are soft. Bonds are under pressure. Yields in Europe are mostly 3-5 bps higher, and the 10-year US Treasury yield (US10Y) is firm near 4.56%. It settled a little below 4.48% at the end of last week. Gold set a record high today (~$2947). After posting a bullish outside up day yesterday, the April WTI is extended its rally today. The contract held $70 yesterday and is now trading above the 20-day moving average (~$72.15). Last week's high was near $73.35.

USD: The Dollar Index has forged a little shelf near 106.55, but the upticks so far have not been impressive. A close today above the five-day moving average (~106.95), which it has not done in over a week, would be helpful, but it needs to rise above the 107.30-55 area to be of technical importance. Today's data may not be particularly helpful. December housing starts soared by a heady 15.8% and that pace is unsustainable. Look for a decline of 6.5%-7.0% in January's print, while permits likely declined for the second consecutive month. Permits fell in eight of last year's 12-months. The average monthly decline in 2024 was 0.2%, after rising by an average of 0.9% a month in 2023. The FOMC minutes may attract more attention, but the market accepts the Fed's "patient" forward guidance. Subsequent official comments suggest that the next Summary of Economic Projections may see the median dot shift from two cuts this year to one. So far, talk in some quarters of the next move being a hike may not be validated in the minutes.

Euro: The euro peaked before the weekend near $1.0515, shy of the January high (~$1.0535) and appears to be retracing the last leg up that began from around $1.0280 on February 10. Yesterday, it settled below the five-day moving average for the first time since then. The euro slipped through $1.0425 in the European morning, which corresponds to the (38.2%) retracement of those gains. Below there, support is seen around $1.04. A break of $1.0370 reinforces our sense that a near-term high is in place. Outside of the aggregate December current account (38.5 bln euros, up from 28.7 bln euros in December 2023), the economic calendar is light. Germany's national election is Sunday, but it does not seem much of a market factor. The CDU is likely to win a plurality of votes. The AfD appears to have been dealt a setback in the polls since it received the endorsement of US Vice President Vance and Musk. The SPD may come in second place. A CDU-SPD coalition seems like the most likely outcome.

CNY: The broadly firmer US dollar has seen it rise toward CNH7.2930, a four day-high. Since the beginning of December 2024, the greenback has mostly traded between CNH7.2350 and CNH7.3700. Meanwhile, the PBOC continues to set the dollar's reference rate in a narrow range between roughly CNY7.1690 and CNY7.1720. Today's fix was at CNY7.1705. China reported that new home prices were fell by 0.07% in January, the first least since June 2023. Used home prices slipped by 0.34%, slightly faster than in December. They last rose in April 2023.

JPY: The dollar peaked last week near JPY154.80 and reached JPY151.25 yesterday. The firmer US rates helped the greenback stabilize, but it held below Monday's high around JPY152.40, which is still holding today. At the same time, the JPY151.25-35 low set this week is holding. Options for $1.35 bln at JPY152 expire today. The January trade balance was true to the strong seasonal pattern for it to deteriorate in January. The shortfall of JPY2.76 trillion (~$18.1 bln) was the largest in two years. Imports rose 16.7% from a year ago, led by communication equipment and computers. Exports increased by 7.2% fueled by autos and ships. Exports to the US rose by 8.1%, with a sharp jump in auto exports, perhaps ahead of tariffs. Japan recorded a JPY477 bln trade surplus with the US last month. Exports to China were off 6.2%. After the stronger than expected Q4 GDP, the December core machine orders, which fell by 1.2% (after a 3.4% gain in November), drew little attention.

GBP: With a brief violation in thin trading on Monday and again today, sterling remains confined to the range set last Friday (~$1.2550-$1.2630). It reached $1.2640 today but is back below $1.26 in late European morning turnover. It has hovered around $1.2610, the (38.2%) retracement of the decline from last September, that began near $1.3435. Since falling by about 10% from there through mid-January, it has appreciated by about 4.35%. The momentum indicators are extended but have not turned down. Still, the correction looks advanced, and a break of $1.2550 may be the first indication that it may be over. The UK reported January CPI earlier today. Due to the base effect, the 0.1% decline in the headline rate translates to a 3.0% year-over-year rate, up from 2.5% at the end of 2024. Service price inflation accelerated (5.0% vs. 4.4%) and the core rose (3.9% vs 3.2%). After yesterday's news of stronger wage growth, there is practically no chance of another BOE rate cut next month (March 20), but the swaps market still favors a cut at the first meeting in Q2 (May 8), but the odds have been shaved to about 85% from 90% yesterday and 100% a week ago.

CAD: The firmer than expected underlying core measures of Canada's January CPI saw the market downgrade the chances of at next month's meeting to about 35% from almost 50% on Monday. We don't think the CPI is the key to the March 12 decision, which comes the same day as the steel and aluminum tariffs the US has threatened are to be implemented and a little more than a week after the end of the one-month postponement granted to the 25% tariffs on all of Canada's exports to the US with the exception of a 10% tariff on energy. The threat of the tariffs is already having a cooling effect, in accord to anecdotal reports. With parliament still not in session, it puts more of the burden on monetary policy rather than fiscal policy in the first instance to address what could be a serious economic shock. The US dollar recorded the session high, slightly below CAD1.4215 before Canada's CPI yesterday. It slipped below CAD1.4180, yesterday's low in pre-European turnover today but is bid in the European morning to push back above CAD1.4200.

AUD: The Australian dollar chopped between about $0.6340 and $0.6360 in the North American session yesterday. It reached $0.6370 today but has been pulled back to the $0.6355 area. The market took the well-anticipated rate cut in stride and shaved the odds of a follow-up cut in July (but still above 90%). The Aussie continues to hold up well even though the upside momentum appears to have stalled after recovering from the push below $0.6090 on February 3 amid the US-sparked tariff panic to a high near $0.6375 earlier this week. For three sessions now, it has settled within a few ticks of $0.6355. The price action could still be a bullish pennant or flag pattern, but a break of $0.6330, and especially $0.6300 weaken the technical tone. Australia's January employment report is due tomorrow. Employment growth is expected to slow. The 56.3k increase in December's employment was fully accounted for by part-time workers, as full-time employment fell nearly 24k. Full-time employment in Australia has not fallen in consecutive months since the pandemic. That said, the unemployment rate is expected to have risen to 4.1% after rising to 4.0% in December from 3.9% in November. Lastly, we note that the Reserve Bank of New Zealand delivered the much anticipated 50 bp rate cut, the third in a row (new cash rate target is 3.75%). It signaled a slower pace of cuts going forward, with the target rate near 3.15% by the end of the year. Counter-intuitively, the New Zealand dollar is the strongest among the G10 currencies today, up about 0.25%.

MXN: The US dollar fell to new three-week lows against the Mexican peso to nearly MXN20.20 today. Last month's low was near MXN20.1350. It has not been below MXN20.00 for more than three months. The daily momentum indicators are getting stretched. This provides a good set-up for a low-risk opportunity to sell pesos as the March 3 tariff postponement comes into view. Mexico's central bank publishes its inflation report later today. While perhaps not completely satisfied, it must be pleased that inflation, both headline and core CPI, are back within the target range (3% +/- 1%). It cut its overnight rate target by 50 bps earlier this month to 9.50% and kept the door open to a similar move at its meeting next month.

data.title: The Long-Term Upside For Chemours Company After Q4 2024
data.description:
Dear readers/followers,

In this article, I'll be taking a look at the chemical company Chemours (NYSE:CC), a business that I have been covering for years, and investing in for some time as well. My last article was in September of last year 2024, which coincided with some accounting irregularities and other headwinds that, following the company's report, led to a decline in the share price which has persisted since then.

I am no stranger to investing in companies during a turnaround - as should be evidenced by my work and articles in the financial sector, including companies like Unum (UNM) and Lincoln National (LNC), one of which has recovered fully and the second of which is showing good promise in doing so in the future.

So a decline like this, provided that the company's fundamentals and that my long-term returns are safeguarded, isn't something that overly bothers me. But again, returns and fundamentals have to be good.

For Chemours, I have believed that returns were, and continue to be good. The recovery may (and should) be more complex because Chemours is a chemical company, and chemical companies are not exactly in favor of the market at this time.

In fact, far from only being a chemical company, the company is a leader in some of its markets. Chemours primary segment, meaning the company is known most for its work in the titanium dioxide sector or TiO2. It has other segments, but this is one of the more significant ones. This is "stuff" that is used in a variety of end-markets, including coatings, various types of packages, and various plastics.

Aside from that, in previous articles, the most recent of which you can find here, I have pointed to the company's role in new refrigerants, its Opteon refrigerant chief among them due to changes in regulation.

The returns since my last article haven't been good, the company is down further, but in the long term, this doesn't bother me all that much. Let's see what earnings at this time tell us.

Chemours - After 4Q24 and going into 2025E, I see an even higher upside for Chemours

Despite the ongoing volatility in Chemours, I view this company as a buying potential. The reason for it, even if 4Q24 hadn't come in, can be seen in the underlying trends for the company over the past few quarters and beyond. While Chemours cannot be argued to be one of the more stable or safe chemical companies at this time, it's certainly one with the most upward potential, if you're willing to bear the risk.

Why do I say this?

The company, during 2024, has seen so far and up to 4Q, strong adoption of its Opteon Refrigerants, over 20% growth in a single year, while also at the same time, providing significant growth in its performance solution segments. Titanium dioxide remains the key to the company's growth, and even that, up until the end of 2024, has been solid - at least in terms of sales volumes. The company's challenges and issues have been primarily price/mix related, not because customers are looking to buy fewer products.

The company has also seen impacts due to its transformation plan. But the downturn has generally expressed itself in declining sales (not volume) as well as downward-trending margins.

However, this hasn't impacted liquidity as much as we would expect it to. In fact, net debt at around $3.5B means that net leverage is below 4.4x and improving, with a cash balance of over $600M and total liquidity of over $1.1B. The potential for something truly disastrous seems to me, even as of 4Q24, to be very remote - and this is in turn what guides my thesis and expectations for this company.

One of the dangers for the company has been where its dividends are likely to go here. So let's talk about that safety for a moment or two. The company has stuck to its $1/share dividend for over 5 years at this point. They have done so as the company's earnings have been below $2/share before, back in 2020. That being said, in no year are the company's earnings forecasted as being as low as during 2024, with a current forecast of $1.22/share, which would imply a payout ratio of close to 82%. This is above the company's typical capital allocation. However, current market forecasts do not consider a cut likely (Source: F.A.S.T graphs paywalled link), nor do I consider it likely. However, I also don't consider a raise likely even when the company starts going better - because, again, the company did not raise it when the earnings moved above $4/share. Because the dividend is still covered, and because I interpret the company's sticking to its dividend as a sign of not wanting to cut it, as well as the company reiterating its importance of distributing quarterly dividends. (Source: CC earnings call) I therefore consider the dividend safe under these conditions.

For 4Q24, the results and impacts were as follows. The company generated higher net sales and EBITDA, fully exceeding both my own and what I can see from forecast expectations, with a 23% 4Q24 YoY net sales growth, coming to 14% yearly YoY net sales growth in refrigerants, one of the growth segments, with Titanium Technologies also up, around a 20% 4Q YoY effect and 8% over the year. The company is continuing to work on its overall asset optimization strategy, but current results for the quarter, which came out less than 40 minutes ago as I am writing this article (and before market open), are good.

EBITDA effect may not show it as much, but this is what is going on "under the hood", even if pricing is continuing to decline slightly, with the company making up the difference with efficiencies, volumes, and some FX.

Price continues to be the primary impact for the company. I actually believe that this is a good thing because together with FX, it's the things that the company cannot exercise much control over. From a segment-by-segment perspective, every single segment saw improvement compared to the YoY period, which is another strong segment for the quarter. While annual comparisons are less favorable, this reflects what I believe to be the ongoing effort to turn things around.

And, as I mentioned above, the company's fundamental in the way of liquidity continues to be solid, even higher in 4Q than in 3Q of this year, with an improvement of almost $100M in liquidity.

Going forward, I work from the following assumptions for Chemours. I believe the non-core segments outside of TT are going to be delivering higher growth than TT, which I continue to expect to decline in the near term, driven by negative sales mix/pricing, with volumes only barely able to keep things stable - at best. This also doesn't take into account upcoming downtime in sites due to weather effects, which is expected to impact in early 2025. Even with positives from the other segments from growth in refrigerants, the overall expectation I have is an at-best flat development in sales, with flat or declining numbers in EBITDA. The company is unlikely to see a major shift in its earnings until TT turns around, and for now, I believe that to be some time off. The company requires more favorable environments for TT and APM, as evidenced by their own forecast.

Based on these results, I say that my long-term case for Chemours is still very much there, but the short-term expectations may need to be tempered somewhat, which is why my valuation now is as follows.

Valuation for Chemours - The upside is there, despite and because of 4Q24

In my last article, I made a continued case for the valuation being good towards a PT of $35 - meaning that at any valuation below $35/share, I view the company as being a compelling investment, if one with a speculative air and one requiring a longer timeframe. I maintain this view as of now.

This article marks the introduction of the addition of two time-based outlook specifications in my thesis/investment case. It's my ambition to start to use these for all companies I review, to clarify my stance on both the short and the medium/longer-term case. With Chemours, as you can read below, the case is longer-term oriented.

What this means is that I don't think you should consider investing for the short term because, despite the likelihood of EPS growth as early as 2025, I see the continued pressure in the segment combined with company-specific irregularities and pressures as not unlikely to contribute to continued volatility.

But if you can stand a bit of up and downs in exchange for a good, near-6% yield, then a 20-30% upside even at a very low sub-9x P/E is entirely possible here.

Chemours has long been traded at low multiples. It should, in my view, continue to trade at low multiples given the risk profile for the company even in the longer term. This is accurately reflected in the BB- credit rating and the multiple for the company. The closest peers that I would consider relevant are businesses like BASF (OTCQX:BASFY), which has outperformed CC over the past year, and is likely to continue to perform well. But CC has a better yield and, to me, a clearer long-term upside potential than BASF.

If we were to look at the company's $16.6/share price today through the lens of 2027 estimates, that would come to a P/E of less than 5x. Because I believe a step-by-step recovery for Chemours to be likely, and because that assumption is backed by historical data that sees this company outperform forecasts and targets 50% of the time on a 1-year basis even with a 10% margin of error, I believe Chemours is now "cheap enough" from a valuation standpoint, both for historical data, as well as peers, which trade far above 4-7x P/E.

That being said, Chemours will neither be my largest nor highest-conviction buy in the sector, merely a "good" one. But a good buy in this market, where cheaper options are few and far between (in my view), is certainly worth highlighting.

So with that in mind, my thesis for Chemours as of February 2025, is as follows.

Thesis

  • Chemours company is fundamentally appealing due to its chemical portfolio but is still impacted by potential legal issues and risks - both future and historical, as well as a liability profile that some consider unappealing. This needs to be discounted, but it's entirely possible to do so - just keep your targets 9x P/E and a share price of $35/share, adjusted for the new information, but remove some of that discount due to a higher current upside.
  • I keep CC as a "Buy" and "Bullish" rating, with an overall price target of $35, below the current analyst average, but considered fair on a peer and risk/reward comparison. As of February 2025, I am sticking to my target here, and now expecting a good 2-3 year trend, with the recovery of TT and other parts towards early or later 2025E - as in my last article.
  • I do clearly maintain a "speculative" rating on the stock, however, and I would not make this a core position in any portfolio at this time.

My short-term investment case (6-12 months) is as follows:

Based on continued volatility in the basic materials/commodities market, the recent irregularities/historical liabilities, and despite the company's strong fundamentals, I foresee the potential/risk for a drawdown as much as the potential for a recovery. Despite 2025E being forecasted to be a very solid year, I see a risk for the company continuing to trail earnings, with a BB- rating and high debt. While I do not consider the dividend to be in danger, I wouldn't count on recovery in the near term.

My medium-to-long-term investment case (up to and beyond 5 years) is as follows:

For the longer term, I believe value investors buying now can count on a higher likelihood of reward. A recovery in commodities and the chemical and specialty sectors as well as currently forecasted rates of growth going above the 20% per year are likely to restore the earnings levels of 2022-2023. If that happens, and the company moves up correspondingly, 20-30% annualized RoR is possible over the longer term, ideally towards 2026-2027.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

As things stand now, the company is still a "BUY", and it fulfills every criterion that I have except one - the quality, due to its non-IG-rating. However, it's very "speculative", and that needs to be kept in mind. This is reflected by me now giving it an overall qualitative rating.

data.title: Bitcoin: The Brave Will Be Rewarded - Maintain A Strong Buy
data.description:
Introduction

Since my initiation, Bitcoin (BTC-USD) has performed by +67% versus +10% for the S&P 500 (SPX). However, for about three months now, BTC has been ranging between $91,000 and $109,000. As I'm writing this article, it stands at $95,641.

In this logic, my last recommendation published on January 7, 2025, and which was my 3rd update on the matter, hasn't performed well.

Since I published it, BTC has been down -1.2% while the SPX has been up +3.48%. In the article that I invite you to look at, I mentioned a major catalyst that I expect to support BTC in 2025.

Despite these positive triggers I have identified, it is undeniable that the current range we are sitting in has brought a wave of pessimism among many investors. As a matter of fact, the BTC fear and greed index is now back in neutral territory.

In this article, I'm going to explain why I believe this situation represents a massive opportunity for investors to initiate or strengthen their BTC exposure.

U.S. States Adoption

Recently, we've seen many bills related to the establishment of a Strategic Bitcoin Reserve introduced in multiple U.S. states. As of today, 22 bills have been introduced in 22 different states. These states will soon have the responsibility to decide if they want to have the possibility to invest in BTC.

The process is long and can take anywhere from a few months to over a year.

After a bill is introduced in the state legislature, it gets debated in the first legislative committee. Then, it moves to the first chamber, where it is debated and voted on. If approved, the bill goes to a second committee in the other chamber for review. There, it is debated and voted on again. If both chambers pass the bill, it is sent to the governor for signing into law.

According to VanEck, if voted across all states, this could potentially represent 250 000 BTC purchased in the future. This is almost 1.15% of the total supply and 1.3% of the circulating supply.

In my opinion, these calculations must be considered carefully, as there is a high probability that not all states will cross the line and adopt BTC.

However, I still interpret this trend very positively. It proves BTC has come a long way and is now even being considered for an investment envelope that requires the highest level of scrutiny: state funds investing citizens money.

UAE - The First Country Checked Off my 2025 Bingo list... and More Will Join The Party

In my previous article, I shared my 2025 Bingo list, where I identified countries, I expected to embrace BTC this year. Among them, I notably mentioned Switzerland, Japan, the United Arab Emirates, and South American countries like Argentina.

A few days ago, an interesting news report came out.

According to Yahoo Finance: Abu Dhabi's sovereign wealth fund, Mubadala Investment Company, disclosed a $460 million stake in BlackRock's Bitcoin ETF, making it the seventh-largest holder of this ETF. In my opinion, this is just the beginning.

Now that the UAE has crossed the line, I expect another country to get in the game soon: Saudi Arabia. Why? Because these countries have historically competed, especially when it comes to investing. A few examples: both have aggressively deployed capital in the technology sector, both have fought to become the financial hub of the Middle East, and even in sports and entertainment, the UAE was the first to make significant investments in European football clubs, while Saudi Arabia followed by acquiring Newcastle United.

In my opinion, with Abu Dhabi's sovereign wealth fund now investing in Bitcoin ETFs, it's only a matter of time before Saudi Arabia follows.

Saudi Arabia's Public Investment Fund manages over $900 billion in assets, making it the 6th largest sovereign wealth fund globally. BTC aligns with the nation's goal of diversifying beyond oil. That's why I believe this country could be a potential buyer, and a game-changer for BTC.

What About the US Strategic Reserve?

First, an important element to consider: the U.S. still holds 207,189 BTC.

There was a non-zero probability that the Biden administration would sell BTC holdings before Trump took office, but it didn't happen. This is very positive, as it suggests a potential bipartisan consensus on the matter.

Since Trump has taken office, we haven't had any specific news about the so-called U.S. strategic reserve. However, another positive development has emerged. On February 3rd, President Trump signed an executive order for the creation of a Sovereign Wealth Fund. This comes along with the task assigned to Crypto Czar David Sacks to study the feasibility of a Bitcoin reserve or a digital asset stockpile.

In a sense, I believe these two elements are related and could both serve as positive catalysts for BTC. It seems plausible that the U.S. sovereign wealth fund, which is expected to total $5.7 trillion in assets, could also invest in Bitcoin, just like the UAE did. Trump has given 90 days to the Secretary of Commerce and the Secretary of the Treasury to create this fund, so we will have news soon enough.

In my opinion, these two developments represent real catalysts that will support BTC and strengthen its reputation as a reserve asset.

Speculation Rising over Fort Knox

Built in 1930, Fort Knox serves as the U.S. Bullion Depository, housing 147 million ounces of gold, worth approximately $425 billion. As gold prices rise and the Department of Government and Efficiency (DOGE) shakes up the U.S. administration, public interest in Fort Knox's actual reserves has surged.

Due to growing distrust over how poorly U.S. taxpayers' money has been managed over the past decade, many are questioning whether the reported gold reserves are accurate. This skepticism has only been reinforced by multiple sources alleging that the last official audit of Fort Knox took place back in 1974.

You might be wondering: how does this relate to BTC? If an audit is indeed conducted and reveals that gold reserves are missing, I have the conviction that it will reignite the gold vs. BTC debate. The idea of gold as the ultimate store of value would take a hit, and BTC, with its verifiable and transparent supply, could emerge as an even stronger alternative.

In a world where trust in traditional assets is eroding, BTC's appeal as a decentralized, non-manipulable form of wealth storage could gain significant traction.

To close this article, I wanted to share this data that, I believe, is crucial. It shows how the ETF and financialization of BTC represent a real advantage for BTC holders, driving demand and making BTC an even scarcer asset over the long term.

Indeed, as time goes by and halving makes it even harder to mine BTC every four years, the new BTC supply continues to decrease. Meanwhile, ETF demand appears strong even a year after their launch. As you can see, despite BTC's poor price action on a YTD basis, net inflows into U.S. spot BTC ETFs have outweighed new supply in 2025 by a factor of 2.68x.

Meanwhile, BTC reserves on exchanges continue to decrease and currently stand at 2.23 million.

Conclusion

Despite the current price action disappointing many BTC holders, I still believe there is significant upside potential in 2025, with major triggers and catalysts on the horizon. I consider the current level attractive, and I remain particularly optimistic about BTC's future.

Periods of consolidation often test investor patience, but history has shown that Bitcoin tends to move in strong cycles, with explosive price action following phases of accumulation. Given the current setup, I have the conviction that we are in such a phase, making this an attractive time to accumulate for those with a long-term view.

The braves will be rewarded. I remain optimistic about BTC's future and maintain my Strong Buy rating.

data.title: S&P 500 Records All-Time Highs Led By Chipmakers, Dow Jones Seesaw Continues
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

The three major Wall Street indices fluctuated throughout the US session, shifting between gains and losses. A rise in chipmakers propelled the S&P 500 to fresh all-time highs late in the session despite a drop in technology stocks.

The S&P 500 edged up by 0.2%, while the Nasdaq 100 also posted a 0.2% gain. The Dow Jones Industrial Average showed mixed movements throughout the session. Meanwhile, the chipmakers index surged by 1.7%.

SPX 500 Top Movers

Intel’s (INTC) stock climbed on rumors about a possible company breakup. Super Micro Computer saw a big jump thanks to a positive outlook. Walgreens Boots Alliance rose after CNBC reported that Sycamore Partners might still be planning a takeover. Meanwhile, Meta’s 20-day streak of gains came to an end.

More Tariffs Ahead?

Yesterday we heard more from President Trump on proposed tariffs that may be announced. President Trump said we should see some announcements in the coming week, while once again lamenting that the EU has been an unfair trading partner.

The President touched on specific sectors, with Pharmaceuticals and Automobiles both expected to get hit with tariffs of around 25%. Tuesday’s comments are his most detailed yet in specifying other sectors that would be hit with fresh barriers.

It will be interesting to see what effect such announcements may have on stock prices moving forward following what has been quite an impressive earning season for US companies.

FOMC Minute and PMI Data Ahead

It is fair to say that following last week’s uptick in US inflation and drop off in retail sales numbers, market participants are once more focused on the Fed’s next moves. The FOMC minutes will be released later today, these should provide further insights into how the Fed sees tariffs impacting their monetary policy course and objectives moving forward.

The week will wrap up with S&P PMI data, which will give us another glimpse into the performance of the US economy from both a manufacturing and service perspective.

Technical Analysis

S&P 500

From a technical standpoint, the S&P has printed a fresh all-time high and could be due a pullback soon.

There was a triangle breakout which occurred as far back as September 19, with a target price of around 6170, which is now just 40 points away.

If we reach this level, I do think we could be in for a short-term correction, however, I expect such a move to be met with buying pressure if the current status quo remains the same.

It is hard to find key levels as we continue to trade at fresh highs with no historical price action to serve as a guide.

However, immediate focus for me will be 6170 and 6200 on the upside.

If price pushes lower from here, support may be found at 6100, 6080 (20-day MA).

S&P 500 Daily Chart, February 19, 2025

  • 6050

Resistance

  • 6170

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Nike's First-Ever Collab Lifts Shares
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Nike (NKE) rallies after picking Kim Kardashian as its first-ever partner for a new brand. (00:23) Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) to pay €326M to settle tax case in Italy - report. (01:17) La-Z-Boy (LZB) sets cautious outlook based on soft housing market. (01:46)

This is an abridged transcript.

Nike (NYSE:NKE) was the biggest gainer in the Dow Jones Industrial Average on Tuesday after announcing that a new brand, called NikeSKIMS, will launch this spring in the U.S.

The collaboration with Kim Kardashian's SKIMS will include training apparel, footwear, and accessories that Nike says combine the company’s sport science with Skims' "obsession" for the female form.

Notably, the development marks the first time Nike (NYSE:NKE) has partnered with an outside company to launch a new brand.

Analysts have weighed in favorably on the upside for Nike (NKE), especially for the long term, on the view that it can add market share in a growing category.

Jefferies analyst Randal Konik said the firm views the NikeSKIMS partnership favorably because it is a promising step forward in the fitness and activewear market.

Shares of Nike (NKE) gained 6.2% on Tuesday.

Milan's prosecutors said they requested to drop a case against the European unit of Alphabet's (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) Google after the company agreed to pay €326M (about $340M) to settle a tax claim.

Reuters reported that the settlement spans the period between 2015 and 2019 and covers sanctions, penalties and interest, the report added, citing a statement from prosecutors.

Google did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

La-Z-Boy (NYSE:LZB) reported better-than-expected results for the fiscal third quarter, but amid expectations for “continued challenging macro conditions,” the furniture company set conservative guidance that was below Wall Street’s expectations.

Buoyed by a 4% improvement in sales and widening operating margin, La-Z-Boy (NYSE:LZB) realized a 1% increase in profits on a per share basis and a penny above Wall Street’s expectations. Total revenue of $522M was also better than anticipated, driven by a 7% increase in same-store sales.

In the wholesale segment, sales were up 2% to $363M due to gains in the North America market that were partially offset by the impact of what was labeled "significant customer transition," most likely a result of the stronger dollar, or increased prices.

Citing both the lingering challenges in the housing market and broader macro conditions, La-Z-Boy (NYSE:LZB) expects Q4 sales to be in the range of $545M to $565M, compared to $553.5M in the same quarter last year and below the consensus estimate of $566.2M. Operating margin is targeted for 8.5% to 9.5% versus 9.1% last year. These estimates do not assume any negative impact from impending U.S. trade policy.

Finally, the company said it will pay an unchanged quarterly dividend of $0.22 per share on March 14 to shareholders of record as of March 4.

RFK Jr. tells HHS staffers childhood vaccines, psychiatric drugs on his radar

Elon Musk, DOGE score victory against state AGs seeking to block access

Catalyst watch:

  • Notable investor events include BrightView Holdings' (BV) Investor Day and Lam Research's (LRCX) Investor Day.

The announcement is expected to be conducted through a press release and promotional materials rather than a live-streamed keynote. A fourth-generation iPhone SE is widely expected to be featured at the event, although there is speculation about a new MacBook Air, new iPad Air, or a surprise reveal.

Now let’s take a look at the markets ahead of the opening bell. Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the red. Crude oil is up 0.7% at nearly $72/barrel. Bitcoin is up 0.5% at $96,000.

In the world markets, the FTSE 100 is down 0.2% and the DAX is down 0.5%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Bumble (NASDAQ:BMBL) shares plunged 18% after Q4 results revealed a decline in Total Average Revenue per Paying User (ARPPU) to $20.58 from $22.64 a year ago, with Q1 2025 revenue projected to decline 7%-10% Y/Y to $242M-$248M, falling short of the $255.48M consensus.
