data.title: Can Earnings Growth Offset Trade Headwinds For Canada?
data.description:
While there is no shortage of risks facing the Canadian market, the TSX is not far from all-time highs. MoneyTalk’s Greg Bonnell discusses how fundamentals for Canadian stocks are looking with Jennifer Nowski, Managing Director and Portfolio Manager at TD Asset Management.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: While there are no shortage of risks globally and here at home, the TSX Composite Index is not far from all-time highs. Joining us now to discuss how to look for where things may go from here is Jennifer Nowski, Managing Director and Portfolio Manager with TD Asset Management. Great to have you back on the program as always, Jennifer.

And yeah, we constantly talk about the risks. We see them in the headlines. But we take a look at the TSX Composite Index. We're not that far from the best we've ever had, in terms of where we're at. I'm rambling. Let's get to you. Where are the Canadian markets right now?

Jennifer Nowski: Yeah. So thanks for having me on. So actually, coming into this year, the TSX had a couple tailwinds behind it in the form of better expected earnings growth and the positive impact of the Bank of Canada rate cuts. Now, the big risk, outstanding, is the potential for US tariff changes.

Now, as you alluded to, the TSX has been doing pretty well. In 2024, it was up nearly 22% And that was led by the technology, financials, and commodity sector. Turning to 2025, the outlook for earnings on the TSX is actually positive. Earnings are expected to grow about 11%, year over year, this year. And that creates a positive backdrop for the market because over the long term, if earnings are growing, it generally leads the stock market higher.

Now, as we dig into the earnings growth and where it's expected to come from, it is actually fairly broad-based. It does vary a bit between sectors, of course. And one thing you have to consider with TSX earnings is they are, of course, influenced by commodities. And actually, though, if, say, spot gold prices were to stay at their elevated levels, that would be a positive contributor for the gold sector. They would generate some good growth this year.

From a more macro perspective, as we know, the Bank of Canada has been cutting rates, and that will serve to take some of the pressure off consumers and businesses and could lead to better economic activity later in the year. Now, in terms of valuation, yes, the multiple for the TSX has expanded over the past year. The forward multiple, or PE, is now sitting at around 15 and 1/2 times. That's about one turn or so above its long-run average. So it's still within range and not within-- at an extreme level.

Now, the big uncertainty facing the market right now is potential changes to US tariff policy. Now, at this point, it's a very fluid situation. However, the ultimate outcome and impact of that is unknown.

Greg Bonnell: All right. So we have this unknown that's hanging over us. That's a binary-- one of your colleagues earlier in the week said, binary situation. Either it happens, or it doesn't happen. When we talk about that overhang of the tariff threat, how does that play out?

Jennifer Nowski: So I feel, in general, whenever I talk about tariffs, that anything I say goes out of date fairly quickly. But at a high level, the whole tariff negotiation creates two challenges for the market because it creates uncertainty and then, potentially, lower GDP growth later on, depending on what happens.

Now, taking the uncertainty piece of it, the market generally doesn't like uncertainty. It's hard to gauge what could potentially happen. But from a more real economic perspective, it also hinders businesses and consumers from making big decisions. So it could slow near-term growth. The other thing is it can distort, say, near-term earnings or economic data as people and businesses try to perhaps adjust their behaviors in anticipation, perhaps, of tariffs potentially coming.

Now, the ultimate outcome is unknown. But if tariffs were implemented, the sizing, timing, extent-- that will determine the impact. It would be a negative for GDP growth in Canada and a headwind for the US, too, because replacing-- or finding cost-effective substitutes for some imports isn't easy. It could be more costly.

The other thing to bear in mind, though, is there are potential offsets to tariffs. So from a Canadian perspective, that might come in the form of a weaker Canadian dollar or additional fiscal and monetary policy supports. So certainly, the tariff negotiations are a risk worth watching.

Greg Bonnell: Let's talk about that weaker Canadian dollar, because obviously, we have seen our currency pull back amid everything that's going on. How does loonie weakness play into the Canadian investing landscape?

Jennifer Nowski: Yeah, so from a TSX or investing perspective, the weaker Canadian dollar basically supports exporters or companies that earn revenues in US dollars but have a cost basis in Canadian dollars. So if you look at the commodity players, commodity prices are priced in US dollars, so they earn US dollar revenue. And many of them that are listed on the TSX have their operations in Canada. So they benefit from the weaker Canadian dollar supporting the revenue side, all else being equal.

The other impact is more of an accounting translation effect, so for businesses that have US operations and translate that income back into Canadian dollars. So that's in areas like technology, some financials, and industrials.

Now, the businesses that have more of a challenge are those that are importing goods from the US. So for example, grocers might face higher costs, and they'll have to manage how they purchase and how they pass that through the consumer. Now, one thing to bear in mind is that often, companies have hedging programs. So in the near term, some of this doesn't maybe flow through quite as expected or as quickly.

Greg Bonnell: When people think about the Canadian market-- and that's what we're talking about here-- they think about dividend players. Let's talk about the performance of some of the higher dividend-yielding stocks. What are you seeing out there?

Yeah, so 2024 was fairly good for the higher dividend-yielding sectors, with the financials and pipelines actually outperforming the TSX. Utilities and REITs also generated positive returns. But the real laggard was telecom. Now, if we take a look at how dividend yielders performed throughout the year, things generally really picked up in the summer. And that's after we had the initial Bank of Canada rate cuts. And then it became-- it was becoming apparent that the Fed was going to cut rates, too.

So the stocks rallied through the fall, and then, I'd say, leveled out near year-end. And that's because the 10-year yield, which fell, initially, with the rate cuts, rebounded and has been, perhaps, stickier. So when we think about the valuation for the dividend yielders, looking at the direction of that 10-year yield will be a key influence through 2025.

Now, I'll dive into two sectors, the laggard and one of the leaders. So starting with the laggard, and that was telecom, which was down during the year. And really, it was more of an industry issue, with heightened competition pressuring prices, that made revenue growth negligible. Looking forward, there's some concerns that with slower population growth in Canada this year, might be more challenging environment.

While there might be some early signs that pricing is stabilizing, it's still something that investors are watching carefully, and we need to see that sustained for longer. The other concern is more stock-specific within the sector. Some of the players do have to lower their leverage, and investors want to see more progress on that. One thing they could do, potentially, is sell assets, for example.

One area that did much better, though, was the pipelines and midstream sector. And that was a benefit of rates, but also a better macro environment in that industry. Oil prices were range-bound, but that's generally-- that's been a healthy environment for their producer customers. Many of them have hit or near their leverage targets and are in a good position to fund some modest growth.

And there is modest growth opportunities for them in, particularly, connecting LNG facilities with gas supply, as well as providing gas supply for growing power demand, as well as some of the renewables growth that these companies have.
